answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem "Husn o Ishq" ("Beauty and Love") from *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, Beauty (*husn*) is depicted as a radiant, transformative force—likened to lightning, dawn, or divine splendor—that inspires, perfects, and animates Love (*ishq*). The speaker positions Love as its responsive counterpart: the culmination, perfect form, or vital essence that achieves fulfillment through encounter with Beauty.[^1]

Iqbal uses vivid metaphors throughout to illustrate this dynamic interdependence. Beauty overwhelms and elevates Love, much like the sun eclipses the moon or light cloaks a lotus, rendering Love's heart alive within its "torrent."[^2] Love, in turn, finds its nature "spurred toward perfection" by Beauty, drawing sustenance and direction from it, as saplings grow green under its influence.[^3] This relationship culminates in mutual realization: astonishment from Beauty's image births perfected Love, while Love's presence manifests new splendors in the mirror of the self.[^4]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 13 "Husn o Ishq" (translation), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/13-husn-o-ishq/translation/#p-73eab8).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 13 "Husn o Ishq" (translation), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/13-husn-o-ishq/translation/#p-f8c692).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 13 "Husn o Ishq" (translation), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/13-husn-o-ishq/translation/#p-7a49e3).
[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 13 "Husn o Ishq" (translation), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/13-husn-o-ishq/translation/#p-0d606e).
